ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hamburger class (FOODON_00002490); no specific Quarter Pounder with Cheese entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers hamburger as a composite beef sandwich; no branded Quarter Pounder entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Quarter Pounder with Cheese; general food concept not required for this record type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Quarter Pounder with Cheese burger weighs approximately 200 g and constitutes a single meal serving.

nutrient_source.title: Quarter Pounder with Cheese — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Quarter Pounder with Cheese (cr_id G00010007). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for McDonald's Quarter Pounder with Cheese (USDA FDC ID 21318) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Quarter Pounder with Cheese in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 66 is estimated using the analogous composite burger entry (white bread bun + beef patty composite) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The bun contributes the dominant glycaemic driver; the beef and cheese components have negligible GI contribution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × available carbohydrate per serving (22.2 g/100g × 200 g serving = 44.4 g) / 100 = 29.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 427 mg/100g; a single 200 g serving delivers approximately 854 mg sodium, representing 37% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this item; if consumed, request without pickles and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods or sides to keep total meal sodium below 800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 22.2 g/100g with dietary fibre only 1.6 g/100g; the refined sesame bun drives a medium-to-high glycaemic response (estimated GI 66), and sugar at 5.1 g/100g with low fibre amplifies postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this item; if consumed, request a lettuce wrap in place of the bun to substantially reduce the refined carbohydrate load, and avoid pairing with fries or sugar-sweetened beverages.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 4.8 g/100g and trans fat approximately 0.2 g/100g; a 200 g serving delivers roughly 9.6 g saturated fat (48% of the 20 g daily limit) and 0.4 g trans fat, both of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this item due to its combined saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden; if consumed occasionally, limit to half a serving and avoid pairing with fried sides or full-sugar beverages.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) contains arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways; trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) is strongly pro-inflammatory per EULAR dietary guidelines; processed cheese adds saturated fat that promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item; consider a grilled chicken or fish burger as a lower-inflammation alternative, and avoid pickles which contribute additional sodium that worsens RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Quarter Pounder with Cheese is a beef cheeseburger consisting of a 113 g (quarter-pound) 100% beef patty grilled and served on a toasted sesame seed bun with two slices of processed American-style cheese, sliced white onions, dill pickle slices, ketchup, and mustard. It is a flagship product of the McDonald's fast-food chain and is widely available in Singapore as a halal-certified item. The patty is seasoned with salt and pepper and cooked on a flat-top grill, producing a juicy, slightly charred interior with a soft, slightly sweet bun exterior. The flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich, with tangy notes from the pickles and ketchup and a creamy, salty finish from the melted cheese. Per 100 g, the burger delivers 244 kcal, 14.1 g protein, 11.6 g fat, and 22.2 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 427 mg. Clinically, the item is high concern for hypertension (sodium load), type 2 diabetes (refined carbohydrate, low fibre, medium-to-high GI), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory red meat and trans fat). It is not suitable for low-sodium, low-GI, or anti-inflammatory dietary patterns. In Singapore, it is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal and is often paired with french fries and a soft drink, which compounds its glycaemic and sodium burden.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010007, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 21318, McDonald's Quarter Pounder with Cheese) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this adaptation is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database composite burger reference.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (4.2 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.8 g/100g), and trans fat (0.2 g/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 21318 (McDonald's Quarter Pounder with Cheese, SR Legacy) as the HPB Foodo source does not report fatty acid sub-fractions. Values are proportionally consistent with the total fat of 11.6 g/100g reported by HPB. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this item, and detailed vitamin and mineral breakdown for this specific composite fast-food item is not available in the HPB database as of 2026. USDA FDC ID 21318 reports some of these values but the record is for the US formulation; cross-application to the Singapore halal formulation is not defensible without confirmation. 3. This is a Western fast-food item of American origin sold in Singapore; it is not a Singaporean dish and therefore the multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. The food_name is the canonical English name. 4. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Quarter Pounder with Cheese appears in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on the composite burger (white bread bun + beef) reference in Atkinson et al. 2008. 5. Glycemic load per serving = 66 × (22.2 × 200 / 100) / 100 = 66 × 44.4 / 100 = 29.3. 6. Halal certification applies to McDonald's Singapore outlets as of the record creation date; this may vary by outlet or over time.
